draft.introduction: Psalm 42 is a lament of exile—the soul thirsting for God as a deer pants for water. It inaugurates the Elohistic Psalter where “God” replaces “the Lord.”

translation.text: As a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God. My soul thirsts for God, for the living God. When shall I come and appear before God? My tears have been my food day and night, while they say to me all the day long, “Where is your God?”
translation.original_text: ὃν τρόπον ἐπιποθεῖ ἡ ἔλαφος ἐπὶ τὰς πηγὰς τῶν ὑδάτων

translation.text: Why are you cast down, O my soul, and why are you in turmoil within me? Hope in God; for I shall again praise him, my salvation and my God. Deep calls to deep at the roar of your waterfalls; all your breakers and your waves have gone over me.

translator_notes.note: The metaphor of thirsting for God became foundational in Christian mysticism. Augustine, John of the Cross, and the Desert Fathers all built on this image of spiritual desire as bodily craving.
